nativeRange.habitat: The Yellow Clown Goby lives in close association with branching staghorn Acropora corals, hovering among the branches and perching directly on them in lagoons and along outer reefs at depths of roughly 2 to 15 metres. In the wild, individuals are generally encountered in small groups of 5 to 15.

diet.notes: Carnivorous. Generally accepts a varied diet of small meaty foods such as frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, copepods, cyclops, and finely chopped seafood, and most specimens also take quality marine flakes and small pellets in time. Small mouth size and a tendency to be outcompeted by faster swimmers mean multiple small feedings per day, with food drifting near its coral perch, are commonly advised.

avoidWith: other Gobiodon species in small systems

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by JarroNevsbaru, 4000x3000. Sharp full lateral profile of a single vibrant bright-yellow individual against a blue background; whole fish in frame, clearly showing the stocky rounded-fin coral-goby body and lemon-yellow colour. Best hero shot.

Photo by Rickard Zerpe, 2124x1593. Sharp close-up of a PAIR of bright-yellow clown gobies sheltering together, matching the species' social pair/group behaviour; heads-on framing but crisp and vividly coloured.

Photo by Jenny, 818x959. In-situ underwater shot of a vibrant yellow individual perched on reef substrate, showing natural perching behaviour and full body; lower resolution but sharp and correctly coloured.

summary: A small, bright-yellow coral goby from the Western Pacific that perches among Acropora branches. Generally peaceful and widely considered hardy, it is well suited to nano and small reef tanks.
careNotes:
The Yellow Clown Goby (Gobiodon okinawae), also known as the Okinawa Goby or Yellow Coral-goby, is a small coral-associated goby from the Western Pacific, commonly reaching about 3.5 to 4 cm in total length. Its range extends from southern Japan south to the Great Barrier Reef and east to Palau and the Marshall Islands. In the wild it lives in close association with branching staghorn Acropora corals, hovering among the branches and perching directly on them, and is generally encountered in small groups of 5 to 15 individuals.

In captivity the Yellow Clown Goby is widely considered hardy and beginner-friendly. It accepts a wide range of small meaty prepared foods and tolerates standard reef water parameters — around 22 to 26 °C, pH 8.1 to 8.4, a carbonate hardness of 8 to 12 dKH, and a specific gravity of 1.020 to 1.025 — without particular difficulty. It is among the more peaceful fish commonly kept in nano reef aquariums.

Many retailers list a 10 US gallon (roughly 38 L) minimum, but a 75 L system or larger, furnished with ample branching rockwork or small polyp stony (SPS) corals such as Acropora, provides better long-term stability and allows space for a pair. Conspecific aggression can be substantial in tight quarters, so keeping multiple individuals is generally advised only in tanks of roughly 100 L and up, with all fish introduced at the same time.

The species is generally regarded as reef-safe, with two caveats worth noting. It has occasionally been observed nipping at the polyps of small polyp stony corals, and spawning pairs lay their eggs on the undersides of Acropora and similar SPS corals, which can cause localised tissue recession around the egg cluster — the coral typically recovers under good water conditions. Like most gobies, the Yellow Clown Goby has been known to jump, so a tightly fitted lid is generally advised.

Reported lifespans range from roughly 2 to 5 years under typical care to 7 or more years in well-maintained aquariums, with isolated hobbyist and public-aquarium records of specimens reaching 11 to 13 years.
breedingNotes: Has been spawned in home aquariums, though raising fry to maturity is generally considered very difficult. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite of an unusual bidirectional type: any two individuals can form a pair, with the smaller of two males changing to female (or the larger of two females changing to male) as needed. Pairs deposit demersal eggs on the underside of branching SPS corals (typically Acropora), where they are guarded; eggs have been reported to hatch in roughly 5-7 days, after which the planktonic larvae require rotifers and other small live foods. Hobbyist rearing success is rare and survival to adulthood is uncommon.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Followed the gobies collection to the dedicated Yellow Clown Goby product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/clown-goby-yellow). Confirmed: Easy care, peaceful temperament, reef-safe with caution toward SPS polyps, 72-78 F (22-25.5 C), pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025, 10 US gal minimum, 1.5 in max, carnivorous diet (mysis, brine, frozen prep foods), Indo-Pacific origin, spawns readily and lays eggs on coral undersides.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed authority (Sawada, Arai & Abe, 1972), Gobiidae / Gobiiformes, Western Pacific distribution (S Japan to S GBR, Palau, Marshall Islands), reef-associated lagoon habitat among Acropora at 2-15 m, max length 3.5 cm TL, wild temperature 20-25 C (preferred 25-29 C), aggregations of 5-15 individuals, facultative air-breathing, IUCN Least Concern (2018).

additional.name: AquaticCommunity (Yellow Clown Goby care guide)
additional.notes: Cross-checked: max ~1.5 in (4 cm), peaceful toward heterospecifics with aggression toward conspecifics in undersized tanks, reef-safe with caveats on SPS, 10 gal minimum for single fish and 30+ gal for multiples, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, 72-82 F (22-28 C), bidirectional protogynous hermaphrodite, accepts flake plus varied frozen foods, native to E Indian Ocean and W Pacific.

additional.name: AquaHoy / Aquarium Insiders / Saltwater Aquarium Blog (lifespan and breeding cross-check)
additional.notes: Lifespan estimates collated across hobby sources: typical 2-5 years in average care, 5-7 years with excellent care, exceptional records of 11-13 years (Randall & Delbeek 2009). Confirms spawning readily on Acropora undersides with fry hatching in 5-7 days; hobbyist rearing success rare.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs will spawn on the underside of branching SPS corals, though the fry are extremely small and require live foods such as rotifers and copepods. Survival to adulthood in home aquariums is uncommon. Any two individuals can form a pair, as the species is capable of bidirectional sex change.
